title: GET /mcp text/event-stream returns 200 with zero body bytes for ≥6s on both production hosts when the homepage tincan demo is the only joined peer — verified empirically via curl and via scripts/validate-mcp-sse-keepalive.js against ams.truthkit.ai and ams.klappy.dev
content: curl -sSi --max-time 6 -X GET -H "authorization: Bearer $BEARER" -H "mcp-session-id: $SID" -H "accept: text/event-stream" https://ams.truthkit.ai/mcp returns HTTP/2 200 with content-type: text/event-stream and the proper keepalive/CORS/expose-headers, then 0 bytes of body for the full 6-second window before curl times out. Reproduced against ams.klappy.dev with identical result. Inspecting agents/mcp@0.12.3 dist/mcp/index.js lines 224–302 confirms why: the SDK opens a TransformStream, attaches a writer that only writes when the inner DO WebSocket forwards a {type:"cf_mcp_agent_event"} message, and otherwise leaves the stream silent. No leading SSE comment, no heartbeat. With a fresh tincan session that has joined a conversation but has no peers yet (the homepage demo's exact path), nothing ever forwards anything, so the wire stays at zero bytes for the lifetime of the connection. Confidence: established (reproduced 3x across 2 hosts, root cause located in SDK source).

title: iOS Safari/WebKit fetch with Accept: text/event-stream raises TypeError: Load failed when the streaming response sits at zero body bytes — this is the exact red "SSE error / Load failed" frame the operator screenshotted from ams.truthkit.ai/tincan-44rkww/conversations/quiet-hawk-3192 at 11:27 — but it is NOT reproducible in headless Chromium or headless WebKit on Linux
content: The homepage's startSSE catch block (worker/src/homepage.ts ~line 1553) reads: `}).catch((err) => { if (err.name !== 'AbortError') appendError('SSE error', err.message); });`. The "Load failed" string is the WebKit TypeError message for fetch network failures; it surfaces here because the streaming-fetch watchdog in iOS Safari fires on responses whose body produces no DATA frames within its (undocumented) idle threshold. Headless Chromium on this Linux container handled the same zero-byte SSE body without error in two independent test runs (existing scripts/validate-homepage-mint.js passed against the same broken production state during this session). Headless WebKit (Playwright webkit on iPhone viewport + iOS user-agent) also tolerated the empty stream in a 20s window. The reproducer for iOS Safari requires a real iOS device or BrowserStack/Sauce Labs — not available in this container. Implication: substrate-level validation (curl, headless Chromium) is structurally insufficient for this failure class; only an iOS device test or a substrate property whose ABSENCE is a necessary cause of the failure can serve as a CI signal.

title: The existing scripts/validate-homepage-mint.js passed against the broken production state during this session, replicating the curl-claim-done failure pattern recorded in journal/2026-05-05-curl-claim-done-incident.tsv — substrate-and-Chromium verification PASS, real-iOS-Safari outcome FAIL, no regression caught
content: Direct replication of the May 5 incident pattern: validator runs in headless Chromium on Linux, page mints + initializes + creates + joins + opens GET /mcp SSE, captures zero error frames and zero pageerrors, exits 0. Operator's iPhone Safari at the same moment shows the red "SSE error / Load failed" frame. The validator was not ceremonial in intent (it asserted exactly what its docstring claims it asserts), but its rendering engine cannot exercise the failure mode the operator hit. Mitigation per outcome-verification-via-runnable-artifact §"Risks and Failure Modes" #4 ("Validator covers the happy path only"): extend the validator surface with a substrate-level assertion whose ABSENCE is a necessary cause of the iOS failure. That is what scripts/validate-mcp-sse-keepalive.js does — it asserts the GET /mcp response produces a body byte within 2s, which iOS Safari requires.

title: Substrate-property validators are a legitimate intermediate when the actual outcome cannot be reproduced in available infrastructure — the discipline is to be honest in the validator's docstring about what it does and does not prove, not to pretend the substrate property IS the outcome
content: The strongest validator authorable in this container without an iOS device farm asserts: "GET /mcp emits a body byte within SSE_FIRST_BYTE_BUDGET_MS of the response head arriving, and that byte is a valid SSE start (`:` comment, `event:`, or `data:`)." This is a NECESSARY condition for the iOS failure mode to NOT fire — if the byte arrives, WebKit's streaming-fetch watchdog has nothing to fire on. It is not a SUFFICIENT condition (other iOS-specific failures could exist), but it eliminates the necessary-cause that empirical evidence (zero bytes for 6s on the wire) names. The validator's docstring states this explicitly: "If this validator passes, the necessary-cause is eliminated and the iOS failure mode cannot fire from this surface. If it fails, the iOS failure mode IS firing." This is the honest framing. The dishonest framing would be "passes on iOS Safari" — which the validator does not test and cannot claim. Future improvement: when an iOS device farm becomes available (BrowserStack / Sauce Labs / dedicated test device), extend the validator surface with a real-iOS-Safari run; until then, the substrate validator is the best available signal and ships under its honest docstring.

title:
worker/src/mcp.ts handleMcp wraps the SDK's GET /mcp response with wrapSseWithKeepalive, which prepends `:ok

` to the body stream and emits `:keepalive

` every 15s — purely additive, no MCP semantics changed, vodka-thin (one function, one apply site)
content: wrapSseWithKeepalive copies every byte the SDK emits through verbatim, only injecting SSE comment lines (which are spec-mandated to be ignored by clients per https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/server-sent-events.html#parsing-an-event-stream). The SDK still owns dispatch, framing, capabilities, session resolution; this is a transport-robustness layer at the response edge. POST and DELETE responses pass through untouched (the wrapper no-ops on non-SSE / non-200 responses). The function lives in worker/src/mcp.ts immediately above handleMcp; threaded into handleMcp via `if (req.method === "GET") return wrapSseWithKeepalive(sdkResponse);`. Confidence: established (textbook SSE robustness pattern; matches what every production SSE server emits — see e.g. SSE keepalive in nginx ngx_http_v2_module, in Server-Sent-Events spec §"Implementation requirements", in @anthropic-ai/sdk SSE client code). Reversibility: two-way (additive bytes; remove the wrapper call to revert to current behavior). Disconfirmer: if iOS Safari STILL surfaces "Load failed" after this deploys, the necessary-cause hypothesis is wrong and the failure mode is something else (CORS, mixed content, cookie semantics, network condition); plan B is to drop the SSE leg from the homepage and use ams_recv long-poll, which is already shipped as the documented degradation path. Plan B is per fresh-iteration-over-steering.

title: Choosing wrapper-side keepalive injection over homepage-side switch to ams_recv long-poll because the SSE-leg failure affects every MCP consumer (Claude Code, Cursor, Desktop, browser), not just the homepage — fixing the wrapper fixes the failure mode for everyone
content: The homepage tincan demo is the most VISIBLE consumer of the SSE leg, but it is not the only one. Every MCP runtime that takes notifications via the standalone GET /mcp leg (rather than long-polling via ams_recv) has the same exposure. Patching only the homepage leaves the underlying substrate broken for every other client and would surface as the same class of failure when the next consumer comes online. The wrapper-side fix is at the choke point — one transformer applied once, every consumer benefits. ams_recv long-poll remains the documented degradation path for runtimes that cannot consume the SSE leg at all (per worker/src/mcp.ts ams_recv tool description); this fix does not remove that option, it makes the primary path actually work. Per vodka-architecture: the wrapper still stays out of the trust loop, still translates only, still no parsing of opaque token data — the additive bytes are SSE protocol-level, not MCP-level. Per security-as-subscriber-pattern: no new gating, no new branching on content; transport robustness only.

title: NOT patching the SDK itself or filing an upstream agents/mcp PR in this slice — the wrapper-side fix is the lowest-cost path to deploy and an upstream PR would be a separate workstream blocked on Cloudflare review cadence
content: The agents/mcp SDK's GET handler at dist/mcp/index.js lines 224–302 is the upstream root cause: a production SSE handler that does not emit leading or periodic keepalive bytes is missing standard robustness. Filing an upstream issue/PR is the correct long-term move — but the operator's iPhone is broken NOW, and the upstream PR is gated on Cloudflare's review cadence. The wrapper-side fix unblocks the homepage tincan demo today; an upstream PR is queued as a follow-up after this lands and the necessary-cause is empirically confirmed. Reversibility: when/if the SDK adds keepalive natively, the wrapper's wrapSseWithKeepalive becomes a no-op (the leading comment is harmless duplicate; the periodic keepalive is harmless extra) and can be removed in a separate commit. Disconfirmer: if Cloudflare's stance is "SSE keepalive is the consumer's responsibility" (defensible architecturally), the wrapper-side fix is the permanent solution and the upstream PR is wontfix.

title: Future regressions in the GET /mcp SSE leg producing zero body bytes WILL be caught by scripts/validate-mcp-sse-keepalive.js before they reach iOS users — running this validator is part of the ship-discipline for any change touching worker/src/mcp.ts handleMcp or the SDK upgrade path
content: The validator demonstrably FAILS against the broken state (HTTP 200 + text/event-stream + 0 bytes in 2s budget; verified twice — once against ams.truthkit.ai, once against ams.klappy.dev — captured to /home/claude/validator-pre-fix-{truthkit,klappy}.txt). It is therefore not ceremonial per outcome-verification-via-runnable-artifact §"Risks and Failure Modes" #1. After this PR deploys via the Cloudflare Workers Git integration, the validator will run against the deployed state; if the wrapper fix is correct, both hosts will return PASS. Going forward, any agents/mcp version bump or worker/src/mcp.ts handleMcp change must run scripts/validate-mcp-sse-keepalive.js as part of its definition-of-done; a regression returning zero body bytes will be caught at the substrate level before the operator sees it on iOS. Validator is added to the "Existing Artifacts" section of canon/constraints/outcome-verification-via-runnable-artifact.md in a follow-up canon PR (not this PR — code and canon changes ship separately per repo convention).

title: PR open against main with two files (worker/src/mcp.ts wrapper + scripts/validate-mcp-sse-keepalive.js validator) and this journal entry; awaiting operator review and merge — Cloudflare Workers Git integration auto-deploys to ams.klappy.dev and ams.truthkit.ai on merge, at which point a re-run of the validator confirms PASS
content: On merge: (1) Cloudflare Workers Git integration deploys the new worker code to ams.klappy.dev and ams.truthkit.ai (both hosts share the same wrangler config, no per-host gating). (2) Operator (or any party) re-runs `node scripts/validate-mcp-sse-keepalive.js` against both hosts; expected output is "VERDICT: PASS" with first-byte-ms below 100ms (leading `:ok\n\n` flushes immediately on response start). (3) Operator opens ams.truthkit.ai/ on iPhone Safari, clicks Mint in the §A TinCan section, and confirms NO red "SSE error / Load failed" frame appears in the console pane. (4) If iOS Safari still shows the failure after deploy, the necessary-cause hypothesis is wrong; revert to plan B (drop SSE leg from homepage, use ams_recv long-poll). (5) Follow-ups queued: (a) canon-only PR adding scripts/validate-mcp-sse-keepalive.js to outcome-verification-via-runnable-artifact's "Existing Artifacts" section; (b) upstream issue/PR against cloudflare/agents to add keepalive support to the SDK's GET handler at dist/mcp/index.js lines 224–302.
